title: Psalms 58
subtitle: Do You Rulers Indeed Speak Justly?

sections.header: Verses 1–5 — “Even from Birth the Wicked Go Astray”

heb.paragraph: "Do you rulers indeed speak justly (ṣedeq)? Do you judge people with equity?" (v.1). An indictment of corrupt judges. Their hearts devise injustice, their hands mete out violence (v.2). The wicked are like venomous snakes, deaf to the charmer’s tune (vv.3–5). They are corrupt FROM BIRTH — innate wickedness, not learned behaviour.

notes.note: "Do you rulers speak justly? No, in your heart you devise injustice." Corrupt leadership corrupts everything beneath it.
"Even from birth the wicked go astray. Their venom is like the venom of a snake, like a deaf cobra." The deaf-cobra image: immune to reason, resistant to charm. Some evil cannot be talked down.

sections.header: Verses 6–11 — “The Righteous Will Be Glad When They Are Avenged”

heb.paragraph: "Surely there is a reward (pərî) for the righteous; surely there is a God who judges the earth" (v.11). The imprecatory section: break their teeth (v.6), let them vanish like water (v.7), like a slug that melts (v.8), like a stillborn that never sees the sun (v.8). The violence of the imagery matches the violence of the injustice. The psalm’s final declaration: there IS a God who judges.

notes.note: "Break the teeth of the wicked." The imprecation: David asks God to disarm the predators. Teeth are the lion’s weapon; breaking them renders the attacker harmless.

"Surely there is a reward for the righteous; surely there is a God who judges the earth." The psalm’s conclusion: justice exists because the Judge exists. The "surely" is emphatic — asserting what the corrupt judges deny.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVDo you rulers indeed speak justly? Do you judge people with equity?ESVDo you indeed decree what is right, you gods? Do you judge the children of man uprightly?NIVBefore your pots can feel the heat of the thorns— whether they be green or dry—the wicked will be swept away.ESVSooner than your pots can feel the heat of thorns, whether green or ablaze, may he sweep them away!

rec.text: ""Do you rulers speak justly? No, in your heart you devise injustice." Corrupt leadership corrupts everything beneath it."

rows.label: Corrupt Judges — 58:1–5
rows.text: "Do you rulers speak justly?" Deaf cobras. Injustice from birth.

hebtext.note: "Do you rulers indeed speak justly (ṣedeq)? Do you judge people with equity?" (v.1). An indictment of corrupt judges. Their hearts devise injustice, their hands mete out violence (v.2). The wicked are like venomous snakes, deaf to the charmer’s tune (vv.3–5). They are corrupt FROM BIRTH — innate wickedness, not learned behaviour.

"Surely there is a reward (pərî) for the righteous; surely there is a God who judges the earth" (v.11). The imprecatory section: break their teeth (v.6), let them vanish like water (v.7), like a slug that melts (v.8), like a stillborn that never sees the sun (v.8). The violence of the imagery matches the violence of the injustice. The psalm’s final declaration: there IS a God who judges.

themes.note: Justice peaks at 10: corrupt judges indicted + "God who judges the earth." Judgment: imprecatory imagery matches the injustice. The harshest psalm so far.
